title: Review updates and integration

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A squash policy combines a noisy feature history into one target commit only after current required checks pass on the reviewed head. Your goal is to revise, re-review and integrate a pull request while preserving traceability. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Remotes and synchronization; Code review; Conflict resolution. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Integration applies the project's approved merge, squash, or rebase policy after required review and checks, then verifies the target branch and cleans up references safely. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Different strategies preserve different history, branch protection can change, and green checks may be stale relative to the final commit. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Any merge button produces equivalent history and a green check from an earlier commit remains valid after new changes.

includes: Respond to review, keep evidence current and merge only an approved passing revision.
Revise, re-review and integrate a pull request while preserving traceability.
excludes: Different strategies preserve different history, branch protection can change, and green checks may be stale relative to the final commit.

outcomes: Revise, re-review and integrate a pull request while preserving traceability.

examples.title: Review updates and integration: direct case
examples.situation: A squash policy combines a noisy feature history into one target commit only after current required checks pass on the reviewed head.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A squash policy combines a noisy feature history into one target commit only after current required checks pass on the reviewed head.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Use the declared integration strategy, require checks for the exact reviewed revision, inspect the resulting target, and preserve traceability to the request.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Use the declared integration strategy, require checks for the exact reviewed revision, inspect the resulting target, and preserve traceability to the request.

examples.title: Review updates and integration: boundary case
examples.situation: After merge, the target branch is updated locally and the deployed or built artifact is verified before the feature branch is removed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: After merge, the target branch is updated locally and the deployed or built artifact is verified before the feature branch is removed.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Different strategies preserve different history, branch protection can change, and green checks may be stale relative to the final commit.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Any merge button produces equivalent history and a green check from an earlier commit remains valid after new changes.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Revise, re-review and integrate a pull request while preserving traceability.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any merge button produces equivalent history and a green check from an earlier commit remains valid after new changes.
misconceptions.correction: Use the declared integration strategy, require checks for the exact reviewed revision, inspect the resulting target, and preserve traceability to the request.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Any merge button produces equivalent history and a green check from an earlier commit remains valid after new changes.

analogy.example: An editorial desk can model Review updates and integration: a request defines the change, a draft presents evidence, reviewers comment, and approved work enters the edition.
usefulFor: Connecting issues, bounded changes, actionable review, checks, and controlled integration
limitations: Repository permissions, automated checks, security disclosure, and commit identity require explicit technical controls.

recap.summary: You should now be able to revise, re-review and integrate a pull request while preserving traceability. Keep this boundary in view: Different strategies preserve different history, branch protection can change, and green checks may be stale relative to the final commit.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Review updates and integration without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Any merge button produces equivalent history and a green check from an earlier commit remains valid after new changes.
Apply the model to this case from memory: After merge, the target branch is updated locally and the deployed or built artifact is verified before the feature branch is removed.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Dependency manifests and lockfiles”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
